data.title: Webull authorizes $100M share buyback program
data.description:
• Webull (BULL) on Tuesday said its board approved a share repurchase program to buy back up to $100M of its Class A ordinary shares over the next 12 months.
  • The online brokerage said repurchases may be made from "time to time" through open market purchases, privately negotiated deals, block trades or other permitted methods, depending on market conditions.
  • Webull said it plans to fund the program using existing cash and future cash flow.
  • "It demonstrates our confidence in Webull's long-term outlook, financial position and growth prospects," CEO Anthony Denier said. 
  • "Share repurchase program reflects our balance sheet strength and our ability to return capital to shareholders while maintaining flexibility to continue investing in our growth priorities," said CFO H.C. Wang. 
  • The company said the buyback program may be changed, suspended or ended at any time.
  • Shares up 9% in premarket trading.

data.title: Stock market higher as hopes of peace deal between U.S. and Iran rise
data.description:
Wall Street rose on Tuesday as renewed hopes for a peace deal between Iran and the U.S. lifted sentiment.

The S&P 500 (SP500) +0.3%, the Dow (DJI) +0.4%, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.1%.

Vice President JD Vance, along with the U.S. delegation, is expected to land in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Wednesday for another round of talks, CNN reported.

The Dow was further helped by better-than-expected results from UnitedHealth (UNH) in its Q1 2026 results. The company also increased its full-year earnings outlook.

U.S. Treasury yields edged higher across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 1 basis point to 4.26%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) climbed 2 basis points to 3.75%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) ticked up 0.5 basis points to 4.89%.

Retail sales jumped more than expected in March.

Investors were preparing for economic data releases, including business inventories and the pending home sales index.

data.title: Pitney Bowes reports preliminary Q1 results, raises full-year outlook
data.description:
• Pitney Bowes (PBI) on Tuesday said it expects higher first-quarter profit based on preliminary results and has raised its full-year financial guidance.
  • The company reported preliminary adjusted earnings of about $0.47 per share for the quarter, up from $0.33 a year earlier, while adjusted EBIT rose to about $130 million from $120 million.
  • Preliminary revenue was about $477 million, down 3% from $493 million a year earlier, though the decline narrowed from prior periods.
  • The company generated preliminary free cash flow of about $44 million, compared with a cash outflow of $20 million a year earlier.
  • Pitney Bowes now expects full-year revenue of about $1.83 billion at the midpoint, compared with a prior midpoint of about $1.81 billion.
  • It raised its adjusted EBIT outlook to about $445 million at the midpoint, from about $435 million previously, and adjusted earnings per share at the midpoint of about $1.58 from about $1.50.
  • Free cash flow is now expected at about $362.5 million at the midpoint, versus about $355 million previously.
  • Consensus revenue for Q1 is $460.67M.
  • The company will report full first-quarter results on May 5 and host a conference call on May 6.
  • PBI +6.89% premarket to $14.0997.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: What Kevin Warsh's Fed chair nomination means for investors – Deutsche Bank’s Jim Reid
data.description:
Kevin Warsh steps into the spotlight for his nomination hearing today at 10 a.m. ET, but his path to becoming the next Federal Reserve Chair faces a significant political hurdle, according to Deutsche Bank Research’s Jim Reid.

Republican Senator Thom Tillis has said he will block all Fed nominations while the Department of Justice’s investigation of current Chair Jerome Powell remains active, which could prevent Warsh’s nomination from advancing through the Senate Banking Committee and reaching the full Senate for approval.

While there’s a common perception that new Fed Chairs are swiftly tested by significant market events shortly after taking office, “the historical evidence paints a mixed picture,” the strategist explained.

 

 

 

Reid noted that when Arthur Burns took office in February 1970, a recession had already begun—it wasn’t a market test of new leadership. Similarly, Paul Volcker’s dramatic tightening that triggered a recession was largely the reason he was appointed in the first place, not a sudden crisis testing his resolve.

“The [Alan] Greenspan era is arguably the best example of an early crisis,” according to the Deutsche Bank researcher, as the Black Monday crash of 1987 occurred just two months into his leadership.

However, more recent Fed Chairs have experienced longer honeymoon periods—the first tremors of the financial crisis didn’t emerge until 18 months into [Ben] Bernanke’s term, with the Lehman default coming 2.5 years in, while Covid-19 didn’t strike until two years into Powell’s tenure.

Reid noted that numerous examples show Fed chair changes were not associated with significant market downside.

Looking ahead, Warsh may need to “navigate a path to maintaining market credibility,” given that markets increasingly believe the U.S. doesn’t need rate cuts, which conflicts with his past comments supporting them and the administration’s desire for lower rates, the analyst wrote.

Powell has stated he will remain as Chair Pro Tempore until a successor is confirmed and will stay on the Board of Governors until the investigation concludes, telling reporters, “I have no intention of leaving the board until the investigation is well and truly over, with transparency and finality.”

Reid concluded that this procedural delay might ironically suit all parties for now while the uncertainties of the war continue to unfold.

data.title: Sen. Tillis remains defiant in blocking Warsh vote for Fed chair - report
data.description:
Sen. Thom Tillis (R-NC), a member of the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs, isn't bowing to White House pressure  to advance a vote on Federal Reserve Chair nominee Kevin Warsh out of committee, according to a media report on Tuesday.

Fox Business senior correspondent Charles Gasparino said Tillis is holding his position that he won't allow a full Senate vote on the nominee until the Department of Justice investigation of current Fed Chair Jerome Powell is resolved.

The White House is also reportedly pressing Sen. Majority Leader John Thune to take a procedural move around the committee, "which as of now seems unlikely," Gasparino wrote in a post on X.

Warsh is set to face questions from the Senate in his confirmation hearing, which is scheduled to start at 10 AM ET on Tuesday.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Starlink cuts internet prices to $35 per month for basic residential plan
data.description:
Ahead of SpaceX’s IPO, Starlink’s high-speed internet prices have been cut from $50/month to $35/month for its 100 Mbps residential plan, with no upfront hardware costs.

The company stated that the offer will end on April 30 and will be available for the first four months in selected areas. It added that the service plan prices will return to standard rates after four months.

The satellite internet unit of SpaceX (SPACE) will charge $65 per month for its 200 Mbps residential plan, while its fastest plan, with speeds of up to 400+ Mbps, will cost $105 per month. The company also offers the option to get a free Mini Kit with the 400+ Mbps plan, along with flexible, discounted service plans for travel, camping, RVing, and more across 160+ markets.

According to market research firm Apptopia’s report, SpaceX is expected to go public this summer at around a $1T valuation, and Starlink is the engine that has to justify much of that number.

The research firm highlighted that Starlink generated an estimated $11.4B in revenue last year, with 63% EBITDA margins, and its subscriber base crossed 10M in February.

The U.S. remained the largest single market for the satellite internet service provider, accounting for 37% of active users, while Brazil’s user base surged 450% YoY and Argentina grew 159%.

data.title: Intel rises as BNP Paribas upgrades ahead of earnings
data.description:
Intel (INTC) shares rose 2% in premarket trading on Tuesday as BNP Paribas upgraded the semiconductor giant ahead of its first-quarter earnings results, set to be released on April 23.

“Intel stock is up 86% YTD and no longer trading on fundamentals, in our view,” analyst David O'Connor wrote in a note to clients. “Better 14A datapoints have significantly raised expectations for 14A success. Coupled to this, the take-off of Agentic AI is driving stronger demand for Intel's server chips and translating into high utilization rates and better chip pricing. That said, key going forward is 14A design wins.”

Delving deeper, O'Connor, who raised his rating on Intel to Neutral and upped his price target to $60, said Intel is likely to benefit from increased server CPU demand caused by agentic artificial intelligence.

“Agentic AI is driving very strong demand for server CPUs, with hyperscalers scrambling to secure supply,” O'Connor said. “Despite lagging on products/process capability Intel is a main beneficiary (c.60% server share) and translated in Intel repurchasing Apollo’s 49% stake in its Ireland Fab-34 earlier this month.”

And while O'Connor said his previous concerns that Intel lagged AMD (AMD) and Arm (ARM) in the server market are still valid, they have become “less important” in the current environment, which he expects to last through the rest of the year.

data.title: SunocoCorp LLC raises dividend by 6.2% to $0.9899
data.description:
• SunocoCorp LLC (SUNC) declares $0.9899/share quarterly dividend, 6.2% increase from prior dividend of $0.9317.
  • Forward yield 6.48%
  • Payable May 20; for shareholders of record May 8; ex-div May 8.
  • See SUNC Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Spanish Mountain Gold sells 1.5% royalty to Wheaton Precious Metals for $55M
data.description:
Spanish Mountain Gold (SPAUF) said Tuesday it has entered into a royalty agreement with Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), which will acquire a 1.5% net smelter return royalty on gold and silver produced from the Spanish Mountain Gold project for $55M to be paid in three installments.

Wheaton (WPM) is "pleased to support the advancement of Spanish Mountain, a long‑life gold project in British Columbia, a jurisdiction we know well," President and CEO Haytham Hodaly said. "The project's scale and long‑term potential align with our disciplined approach to growth in established mining jurisdictions."

"Entering into the definitive financing agreement propels the Spanish Mountain Gold project feasibility study forward, which the company aims to complete in 18 months, enabling a build decision in 2028," Spanish Mountain Gold (SPAUF) President and CEO Peter Mah said.

data.title: Intel, Micron, and Nvidia favorites as KeyBanc highlights semiconductor cycle
data.description:
KeyBanc said its favorite ideas include Intel (INTC), Micron Technology (MU), and Nvidia (NVDA) while highlighting its fourth quarter 2025 quarterly semiconductor cycle analysis.

Analysts led by John Vinh said that the semiconductor cycle remains intact as the real cyclical recovery is yet to start. The analysts highlighted the following four points.

Firstly, the analysts said the real cyclical recovery is still to come, as we have just begun to ship above the trendline. "Historically, in cyclical upturns, where 3MMA [three-month moving average] shipments have been above the trendline, the average duration of the recovery has been just over two years or 8.5 quarters. Given that we have yet to ship above the trendline for any meaningful period, this would suggest the broader cyclical upturn ex-AI has yet to commence. Given the historical average recovery cycle duration of two+ years, and the added durability of AI infra build-out as a sustained demand driver, we believe this recovery can extend at least through 2027."

The analysts noted that lagging segments include auto, which has yet to see meaningful inventory restocking or a demand inflection, while industrial has been comparatively stronger. The analysts expect a broader shallow cyclical recovery to strengthen in the upcoming periods and represent additional upside to shipment volumes as those recoveries eventually materialize.

Secondly, Vinh and his team said that total aggregate supply chain inventories decreased one day to 79 days, which is still above the five-year fourth quarter average of 75 days. However, the analysts noted that ex-AI days of inventory, or DOI, has decreased to 75 days, which is in line with normalized historical averages.

"The decline this quarter reflects more disciplined channel behavior, as suppliers have been shipping below end-demand to support continued inventory digestion. Ex-AI, DOI has largely normalized. The decrease was most pronounced at end customers with a more modest decline in semiconductor inventories, while EMS [Electronics Manufacturing Services], ODM [Original Design Manufacturing], and component distributors saw a ~two-day increase in inventories. The one-day decrease in aggregate DOI was in line with the seasonal (five-year average) one-day decline, compared to the 3Q one-day decrease in aggregate DOI, the 2Q two-day decrease, and a two-day q/q increase in 1Q," the analysts added.

Thirdly, the analysts said that fourth quarter integrated circuit, or IC, unit shipments were -2.1% quarter-over-quarter and +3.5% year-over-year. Total fourth quarter IC 3MMA unit sales were 1.3% above the implied 3MMA trendline, tracking slightly above the trendline.

Lastly, Vinh and his team said that they were raising their 2026 IC ex-memory revenue forecast to +22% from +19%, driven by raised pricing assumptions reflecting price increases starting in the second quarter, while unit assumptions are maintained.
